figures.caption: Table 1: Comparison of state-of-the-art methods across video understanding benchmarks on LLaVA-Video-7B. Best results are in bold, second best underlined. “Avg.” indicates the mean performance across all benchmarks, where “score” represents the average score over all benchmarks, and “%” denotes the ratio of this average score to that of the unpruned LLaVA-Video model.

Table 2: Comparison of state-of-the-art methods across video understanding benchmarks on LLaVA-Onevision-7B. Best results are in bold, second best underlined. “Avg.” indicates the mean performance across all benchmarks, where “score” represents the average score over all benchmarks, and “%” denotes the ratio of this average score to that of the unpruned LLaVA-Video model.

Figure 2: Framework of HieraVid. To balance visual information loss from shallow-layer pruning and inefficiency from deep-layer pruning, we apply layer-level multi-stage pruning to the LLM. HieraVid divides the pruning process into three stages. (i) Merge Ratio-guided Segmentation: At the LLM input layer, static tokens at corresponding positions across merged frames are temporally combined by merging similar tokens at their first occurrence. Furthermore, we partition frames into multiple segments based on the merge ratio between adjacent frames to ensure inter-segment diversity and intra-segment continuity (see Figure 3). (ii) Segment Budget Allocation dynamically assigns pruning ratios to each segment to maximize visual diversity after pruning. (iii) Frame-level DPP Pruning integrates the DPP kernel matrix with instruction features to balance visual diversity and instruction relevance in the pruned tokens.
Figure 4: Ablation Experiments on segment threshold 𝛽.
Table 6: Ablation study on 𝜆 for the budget of segment dynamic ratio allocation on LLaVA-Video (Relative Token Budget: 30%). Vanilla denotes the original LLaVA-Video before pruning.

Figure 3: Visualization of frame segment results by our HieraVid. The horizontal dashed line represents the segment threshold. Lower bars indicate lower similarity with preceding frames. Black borders mark the segmentation boundaries, where frames within each segment exhibit high similarity, while those across segments maintain distinctiveness.
